titleStmt.title: Letter from James Tate to his mother, Millicent Tate (née Farren), 14 August 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: James Tate

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p:
Letter from James Tate to his mother in which he refers to a new sailing boat that has arrived to the port which they are using for sailing in the evenings. He also talks about a new ambulance, the cavalry, and technical work for the bridge. He also asks for books and copies of Blackwood Magazine to be sent to him.
James's father, Alec C Tate, also served with British army in India before he married Millicent Farren and started a family in Whiteabbey Co. Antrim. Their sons, James and William continued the military tradition and enlisted with the British army with James serving as a Second Lieutenant with the Indian Expeditionary Force. James spent the majority of the war in India during which time the Indian Expeditionary Force was dispatched to Mesopotamia.
This letter is part of a collection that he wrote to his parents detailing his movements and his day to day experience.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland

correspAction.placeName: No.3 Bridging Train, Indian Expeditionary Force

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
No 3. Bridging Train. I.E.F.D. 14th August. 1916. My dear Mother I was very glad indeed to get your last letter with
the news that Daddy was getting on so well & I hope
he will continue to do so. I hope the rest of the family
are is also doing well. There is nothing much doing
here and I am still very well. The wind has kept
up well and it is getting cooler & cooler each day
The nights are quite cold and I am just going
to start a blanket. A new sailing boat has arrived
for the post in which we go out for sails in the
evening. But as the wind is always straight down
stream we can only go straight accross
home; we usually go straight accross. No Iron
has come for the bridge so we are no nearer
getting away. We are at present enlarging the camp
— a very slow & rotten job. There are going to be
a lot of changes here. The gunners are being
changed. The cavalry hopes to go on leave — they
have been out here well over a year and the
ambulance has changed so we will soon
be the oldest inhabitants if the Field Park
does not hurry up the bridge stuff.
Please tell the cat that we have made a new
arrangement by which Fox orders tins etc.&
ask her to send rather smaller parcels than
I asked her to last letter — Books chiefly
Blackwoods, Magizine & anything she can think of
& also about six tins of sorts a month. I am afraid she will not know whether she is
standing on her head or her heels but still
things have changed in the last week & we
will soon be messing on our own. I hope questionable reading is very well & a very good
Boy and I hope Baby got my letter
with love to you all from your
loving son Jim
Mrs. A.C. Tate
Slieve - na questionable reading 2 Glenlucin
Whiteabbey Ballycastle
Co Antrim
IRELAND
